query: pathophysiology of Dubin-Johnson syndrome and unconjugated bilirubin levels

retrieved_docs.title: Dubin-Johnson Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: In 1954, Dubin and Johnson described a new clinicopathological entity consisting of chronic idiopathic jaundice with unidentified pigment in the liver in 12 cases. In the same year, Sprinz and Nelson published a report of four cases with persistent non-hemolytic hyperbilirubinemia associated with lipochrome-like pigment in the liver cells; soon, the syndrome had come to be known as Dubin Johnson syndrome (DJS). This relatively rare disorder is a variety of hereditary hyperbilirubinemia and is characterized by low-grade elevation of conjugated bilirubin and no other signs of hepatic injury. This activity describes the pathophysiology of Dubin johnson syndrome and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in its management.

retrieved_docs.title: Dubin-Johnson Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with DJS have hyperbilirubinemia, and it is predominantly conjugated hyperbilirubinemia. Total bilirubin concentrations are typically between 2 and 5 mg/dL but on occasion may rise to 20-25 mg/dL, but this distinctly unusual. Laboratory tests including complete blood count, serum albumin, alanine aminotransferase, aspartate aminotransferase, alkaline phosphatase, cholesterol, and prothrombin time are normal. There is no evidence of hemolysis. Patients with DJS have a diagnostic abnormality in urinary coproporphyrin excretion, and abnormal distribution of the coproporphyrin isomers I and III in the urine is a characteristic feature of DJS. There are two naturally occurring coproporphyrin isomers, I and III. Normally about 75% of the coproporphyrin in urine is isomer III. In patients with DJS, the total coproporphyrin content of the urine is normal, but >80% is isomer I.

retrieved_docs.title: Dubin-Johnson Syndrome -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In 1954, Dubin and Johnson described a new clinicopathological entity consisting of chronic idiopathic jaundice with unidentified pigment in the liver in 12 cases. In the same year, Sprinz and Nelson published a report of four cases with persistent non-hemolytic hyperbilirubinemia associated with lipochrome-like pigment in the liver cells; soon, the syndrome had come to be known as Dubin Johnson syndrome (DJS).  This relatively rare disorder is a variety of hereditary hyperbilirubinemia and is characterized by low-grade elevation of conjugated bilirubin and no other signs of hepatic injury. It results from a mutation that leads to improper excretion of bilirubin from hepatocytes. In most cases, DJS presents in the second decade of life, and rarely it may present in an infant.

retrieved_docs.title: Dubin-Johnson Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Bromosulphthaleine (BSP) clearance test is no longer used in clinical practice. If done in patients with DJS, DSP clearance is normal at 45 minutes, with a second (biphasic) peak occurring at 90 minutes, indicating an excretory defect. The second rise is related to the reflux of conjugated BSP from the liver to plasma. BSP clearance test is not specific for DJS, and similar findings may present in other cholestatic hepatobiliary disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: Jaundice -- Etiology -- CONJUGATED HYPERBILIRUBINEMIA [6]
retrieved_docs.content: Defect of canalicular organic anion transport [7] Dubin-Johnson syndrome

retrieved_docs.title: Dubin-Johnson Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnosis of DJS can be made based on the presence of conjugated hyperbilirubinemia with no other abnormality of liver function tests and elevated urine coproporphyrin I fraction. Invasive diagnostic tests should be avoided. Genotyping of the ABCC2 gene is possible but used for scientific studies and not the clinical purpose. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Dubin-Johnson Syndrome -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Most patients with DJS are often young adults and are asymptomatic. Hyperbilirubinemia is found as an incidental finding while undergoing routine testing or being tested for some other unrelated problem. They may rarely present with mild icterus, weakness, and/or upper abdominal pain. Pruritus is not a symptom of DJS since serum total bile acid levels are normal. In women, the condition may be subclinical and discovered because of hyperbilirubinemia or obvious jaundice once started on oral contraceptives or becoming pregnant, at which time development of jaundice may lead to the establishment of the diagnosis. Except for mild icterus, physical examination results are within normal limits. [7] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Dubin-Johnson Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: DJS comes from mutations of the ATP binding cassette subfamily C member (ABCC2) gene. The ABCC2 gene provides instructions for producing a protein called multidrug resistance protein 2 (MRP2) that works as a transporter protein. The MRP2 protein is involved in the transport of substances out of cells and is essential for the secretion of conjugated bilirubin out of the hepatocytes into the bile duct system for excretion. Mutation of the ABCC2 gene causes a deficiency of canalicular MRP2 expression and impairs the transport of conjugated bilirubin into the bile duct system. Conjugated bilirubin accumulates in hepatocytes, and conjugated bilirubin levels rise in the blood. [2] [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Jaundice -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: AST/ALT ratio is more than 2 to 1 in alcoholic liver disease. AST and ALT values are in 1000s; then the hepatocellular disease is likely due to toxins like acetaminophen or ischemia or viral. If ALP levels are five times elevated than normal and liver transaminases are normal or less than two times normal, then the most likely cause is biliary obstruction. The high serum ALP levels due to a biliary injury can be differentiated from bone disorders by ordering a GGT serum profile, increased levels confirm hepatic origin. If AST, ALT and ALP levels are normal- then the jaundice is not due to liver or bile duct injury. The cause must probably be pre-hepatic: inherited disorders of liver conjugation or blood disorders or defect in hepatic excretion (Rotor, Dubin-Johnson). Serum Bilirubin - whether there is a rise in unconjugated or conjugated bilirubin

retrieved_docs.title: Dubin-Johnson Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Studies such as the hepatobiliary iminodiacetic acid (HIDA) scan and computerized tomography (CT) of the abdomen are performed in patients with hyperbilirubinemia. In the patients with DJS, the HIDA scan shows a characteristic pattern of delayed visualization or no visualization of the gallbladder and bile ducts in the presence of intense, homogenous, and prolonged visualization of the liver. Similarly, oral cholecystography will fail to visualize the gallbladder- subjecting many patients to unnecessary laparoscopic surgery because of a false positive result suggesting the presence of gallstones. Computed tomography scans in patients with DJS show a significantly higher attenuation than those seen in normal subjects. Therefore, these studies generally are not needed or done if DJS is suspected. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Conjugated Hyperbilirubinemia -- Pathophysiology -- Intrahepatic Cholestasis:
retrieved_docs.content: Intrahepatic cholestasis is a hepatocellular condition associated with aberrations in bilirubin transport, bile canalicular membrane fluidity, as well as hepatocyte cytoskeletal function; these deviations caused by a myriad of disease processes with or without bile duct alterations, render the hepatocyte inept in metabolizing and releasing bile. [29] Dubin-Johnson syndrome, one of the inherited causes of intrahepatic cholestasis, results from a defect in multidrug-resistant protein 2 (MRP2) that mediates the transport of conjugated bilirubin into bile resulting in elevated conjugated bilirubin. Its inheritance is autosomal recessive and results in a pigmented (black) liver. [31] Dubin-Johnson syndrome is benign and needs no treatment but is exacerbated by pregnancy and oral contraceptive use. Similarly, Rotor syndrome is an autosomal recessive disorder, characterized by homozygous mutation in SLCO1B1 and SLCO1B3 genes on chromosome 12, resulting in abnormally short organic anion transporting polypeptide 1B1 and 1B3 (OATP1B1 and OATP1B3). [32] A defect in OATP1B1 and OATP1B3, proteins involved in the uptake of conjugated bilirubin in normal hepatocytes, leads to conjugated hyperbilirubinemia. [32] Dubin-Johnson syndrome and Rotor syndrome have many features in common; however, there is an absence of pigmented liver in Rotor syndrome. Dubin-Johnson syndrome is also characterized by the unusual ratio of byproducts of heme biosynthesis. In a healthy subject, the ratio of coproporphyrin 3 to coproporphyrin 1 is around 3-4:1; however, in patients with Dubin-Johnson syndrome, the ratio is reversed. [33] Urinalysis for porphyrins reveals the normal level of coproporphyrin; however, the I isomer is about 80% of the total. Similarly, the gall bladder is visible with oral cholecystogram in Rotor syndrome, and not visible in Dubin-Johnson syndrome. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Unconjugated Hyperbilirubinemia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia is a condition defined as elevated serum or plasma bilirubin (unconjugated) levels above the reference range of the laboratory. Unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia usually results from dysregulation in the bilirubin metabolism that includes increased production, impaired hepatic uptake, and decreased conjugation of bilirubin. In newborns, unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia is very common, and increased bilirubin (unconjugated) levels can cause life-threatening kernicterus. This activity describes the pathophysiology and management of unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia. It also highlights the importance of teamwork in the management of patients with this disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Rotor Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The defective storage and excretion of organic anions, such as bilirubin, lead to impaired secretion of conjugated bilirubin, which leads to its reabsorption into the bloodstream and subsequent excretion in the urine. The resulting hyperbilirubinemia manifests as jaundice. As opposed to Dubin-Johnson syndrome, hepatic pigmentation has not been reported.

retrieved_docs.title: Dubin-Johnson Syndrome -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Dubin-Johnson syndrome is a rare and benign condition but it is not easy to diagnose. the condition is often misdiagnosed with gallstone disease, and patients are unnecessarily subject to surgery. For this reason, patients should be managed by an interprofessional team. It is essential to diagnose the disorder and to eliminate other conditions that may lead to hepatic injury. A team approach is an ideal way to limit invasive testing, proper management when needed, and correct diagnosis. When assessing for Dubin-Johnson syndrome, the patient should have the following done: Evaluated by the gastroenterologist or hepatologist to rule out other potentially harmful disorders Be taught by the nurse of possible precipitating factors Be consulted by the pharmacist for management of severe neonatal Dubin-Johnson syndrome The pharmacist should educate the patient that no drug treatment is necessary and medications like phenobarbital may in fact worsen the hyperbilirubinemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Dubin-Johnson Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: A classic feature of DJS is the reversal of the ratio of the byproducts generated during heme synthesis. The levels of urinary coproporphyrin l levels are greater than coproporphyrin lll. In otherwise healthy people, the ratio of coproporphyrin lll to coproporphyrin l is about 3.5:1.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Liver -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: A common etiology of prehepatic jaundice is hemolysis, where the level of hemolysis overwhelms the conjugating capacity of the liver, resulting in a buildup of unconjugated bilirubin, causing jaundice. Causes of intrahepatic cholestasis can be congenital diseases, such as Gilbert syndrome and Crigler-Najjar syndrome. In these congenital diseases, the enzyme responsible for bilirubin conjugation, UGT, is mildly deficient or completely deficient. Dubin-Johnson and Rotor syndrome are causes of direct bilirubinemia as there is a defect in canalicular transport of conjugated bilirubin. Other causes of post-hepatic cholestasis are an obstruction, such as due to a stone or malignancy. Viral hepatitis can result in both indirect and direct hyperbilirubinemia. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2269
retrieved_docs.content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is a specific form of neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn. This condition is characterized by a spectrum of related neonatal complications. Among them are neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation, which results from a delayed development of the conjugating system, and the broader manifestation described as يرقان نووي (neonatal jaundice). In addition, affected infants may experience نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ, a hemorrhage of the respiratory tract in the newborn. Together, these interrelated features underscore the importance of recognizing Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome within the context of neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease, as they impact bilirubin metabolism, bleeding risk, and overall newborn care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 1288
retrieved_docs.content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is classified within the broader category of neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn. This category also encompasses several distinct forms of neonatal jaundice. One form is neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation that results from the delayed development of the bilirubin‑conjugating system. Additional subtypes include breast‑milk‑induced jaundice (يرقان حليب الثدي), jaundice that arises from immature hepatic conjugation in fetuses or newborns (يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد), and jaundice that is associated with preterm birth (يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان). Together, these conditions represent various manifestations of impaired bilirubin metabolism and hemolysis in the newborn period, highlighting the importance of recognizing specific etiologies for accurate diagnosis and appropriate management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 937
retrieved_docs.content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is classified as a specific form of NEONATAL HEMORRHAGE AND HEMOLYTIC DISEASE OF THE NEWBORN. The broader category of NEONATAL HEMORRHAGE AND HEMOLYTIC DISEASE OF THE NEWBORN encompasses several distinct types of neonatal jaundice, including Neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation from delayed development of the conjugating system, the general condition يرقان نووي (neonatal jaundice), يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد (neonatal jaundice caused by hemolysis in the fetus or newborn), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (neonatal jaundice associated with preterm birth). These interrelated entities reflect different pathophysiological mechanisms—such as hemolysis, immature hepatic conjugation capacity, and prematurity—that can contribute to hemorrhagic and hemolytic disease in the newborn. Recognizing how Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome fits within this spectrum and how the various forms of neonatal jaundice relate to the overarching disease category is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and targeted management of affected infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2344
retrieved_docs.content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is categorized within the broader condition of neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn. Neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn encompasses several related pediatric entities, including neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation from delayed development of the conjugating system, نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory tract hemorrhage), يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (neonatal jaundice associated with preterm birth). These interconnected disorders illustrate the diverse mechanisms—such as impaired bilirubin conjugation, respiratory tract bleeding, milk‑related bilirubin excess, and prematurity‑related metabolic immaturity—that can contribute to hemorrhagic and hemolytic presentations in the newborn, underscoring the need for comprehensive evaluation and targeted management in this vulnerable population.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 1206
retrieved_docs.content: Bacterial sepsis of the newborn (unspecified), Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome, neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation (including the specific form caused by delayed development of the conjugating system), نزيف حديثي الولادة (neonatal hemorrhage), and يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice) are all conditions that occur during the neonatal period. The qualifier “Neonatal” therefore applies to each of these entities, indicating that their onset, presentation, or occurrence is confined to the first weeks of life. Recognizing that these diverse disorders share the common temporal context of the neonatal stage helps clinicians focus diagnostic evaluation and management strategies appropriate for newborn patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1200
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn is linked to a spectrum of neonatal jaundice conditions and related complications. It is associated with neonatal jaundice caused by delayed conjugation, as well as breast‑milk jaundice, physiologic (newborn) jaundice, and jaundice that is specifically related to preterm birth. In addition, this condition can be accompanied by a loose umbilical cord clamp that leads to bleeding. Recognizing these interconnected manifestations is important for comprehensive assessment and management of affected newborns.

Neonatal jaundice manifests in multiple specific forms: neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation, neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation from delayed development of the conjugating system, breast‑milk jaundice (يرقان حليب الثدي), nuclear jaundice (يرقان نووي), nuclear jaundice caused by epidermal immaturity in the fetus or newborn (يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد), physiologic neonatal jaundice (يرقان وليدي) and physiologic neonatal jaundice associated with preterm birth (يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان). retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1081
retrieved_docs.content: NEONATAL HEMORRHAGE AND HEMOLYTIC DISEASE OF THE NEWBORN is a complex neonatal condition that gives rise to several related complications. One important manifestation is neonatal jaundice that results from delayed conjugation due to the immature development of the hepatic conjugating system. In addition, the condition includes نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory tract hemorrhage), which reflects the hemorrhagic component of the disease. Various forms of neonatal jaundice are also linked to this disorder: يرقان نووي (neonatal jaundice) in general, يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد (neonatal jaundice caused by hemolysis of the fetus or newborn), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (jaundice associated with preterm birth). Together, these interrelated entities illustrate how hemorrhagic and hemolytic processes in the newborn can impair liver function, lead to delayed bilirubin conjugation, and produce respiratory bleeding, underscoring the need for comprehensive assessment and coordinated management of affected infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2268
retrieved_docs.content: The neonatal qualifier value encompasses several newborn conditions. It includes umbilical bleeding after birth, which can occur when a loose umbilical cord clamp leads to bleeding. It also occurs in various forms of neonatal jaundice, such as breast‑milk jaundice, physiologic jaundice that results from immature liver function in fetuses or newborns, and jaundice that is linked to premature delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 1076
retrieved_docs.content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is a neonatal disorder that manifests during the neonatal period. In the same neonatal stage, several other conditions are observed, including neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation from delayed development of the conjugating system, نزف سري بعد الولادة (umbilical bleeding after birth), نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory tract bleeding), and يرقان نووي (neonatal jaundice). These entities are all linked by their occurrence in the neonatal timeframe, underscoring the need for clinicians to recognize a spectrum of metabolic, hemorrhagic, and respiratory complications that can arise in newborns for timely and appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1199
retrieved_docs.content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is identified as a neonatal condition, indicating that it manifests during the newborn period. The neonatal qualifier encompasses several distinct clinical entities. Neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation from delayed development of the conjugating system represents a form of early‑life hyperbilirubinemia caused by the immature hepatic capacity to process bilirubin. In addition, neonatal hemorrhage after birth (نزف سري بعد الولادة) and neonatal respiratory tract bleeding (نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ) are recognized as bleeding complications that can occur in the immediate post‑natal period, affecting either the umbilical region or the respiratory passages. Another neonatal manifestation is nuclear jaundice resulting from the protective coating (التمنيع الإسوي) in embryos or newborns (يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد), which reflects a specific type of jaundice linked to fetal or neonatal physiological factors. Together, these entities illustrate the range of disorders and complications that are characteristic of the neonatal stage, emphasizing the importance of early recognition and management of metabolic, hemorrhagic, and respiratory conditions in newborn care.

Laboratory findings that fall below reference ranges, particularly low total hemoglobin concentration, are interpreted as indicative of hemolytic or perinatal jaundice, underscoring the diagnostic importance of quantitative testing in this age group. Collectively, these relationships illustrate that the neonatal period serves as a critical temporal nexus linking infectious, metabolic, hematologic, and hemorrhagic conditions—many organized under “other perinatal conditions” or “hemolytic‑jaundice” categories—and that recognizing these interconnections is essential for accurate assessment, diagnosis, and management of newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 917
retrieved_docs.content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is classified under neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn. This broader category encompasses several specific neonatal conditions, including نزف سري بعد الولادة (umbilical hemorrhage after birth), نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (respiratory tract hemorrhage in the newborn), يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice), and يرقان نووي (physiologic neonatal jaundice). Together, these entities represent key hemorrhagic and hyperbilirubinemic presentations that are important for the diagnosis, monitoring, and management of affected newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 2317
retrieved_docs.content: NEONATAL HEMORRHAGE AND HEMOLYTIC DISEASE OF THE NEWBORN is linked to several serious neonatal complications. It includes نزف سري بعد الولادة (intracranial hemorrhage after birth) and نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory tract hemorrhage) as direct manifestations of the hemorrhagic component. In addition, the condition is associated with يرقان نووي (neonatal jaundice). The neonatal jaundice observed in this context is specifically described as Neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation and further clarified as Neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation from delayed development of the conjugating system, indicating that the immature hepatic conjugation pathway in the newborn leads to accumulation of unconjugated bilirubin. Recognizing the interplay between hemorrhagic events, respiratory bleeding, and delayed‑conjugation jaundice is essential for comprehensive assessment and management of affected newborns.
